title: The closed door of Number 14
summary: A Cairo postman keeps delivering letters to a building no one has lived in for two years — in case someone comes back.
body:
Yusuf is a postman in Cairo. He delivers letters in the same neighbourhood every day. He has done this for twenty-two years.

On his street there is a building called Number 14. The building is closed. The owner went to Canada two years ago. No one lives there now.

But the letters still come. Six or seven a month. Bills. Cards from old friends. A magazine. Sometimes a wedding invitation.

Yusuf could send them back. The post office has a rule for that. He should write 'no longer at this address' and return them.

He does not.

He puts them in a small box behind his counter at the post office. He has a box for Number 14. He also has a box for Number 27, an old man who moved to Alexandria. And a box for the woman from Number 9, who died last winter.

One day, a young woman came to the post office. She was the daughter of the woman from Number 9. She had come from Germany.

Yusuf gave her the box.

She sat on the steps outside. She read each letter. Some made her cry. Some made her smile. After two hours, she came back and said, 'How did you know I would come?'

Yusuf said, 'I did not know. But somebody might. And if nobody comes, the letters are still where they were. That is more honest than the bin.'

annotations.meaningVi: người đưa thư / nhân viên bưu điện
hóa đơn (tiền điện, nước,…) — số nhiều
thiệp cưới / lời mời đám cưới
không còn ở địa chỉ này nữa (câu chuẩn của bưu điện)

grammarNotes.title: Modal 'could' and 'should' for possibility and rules
grammarNotes.bodyVi: 'Could' và 'should' đều là động từ khiếm khuyết. 'Yusuf could send them back' = anh ấy có thể làm việc đó (khả năng). 'He should write no longer at this address' = anh ấy được kỳ vọng làm vậy (theo luật). Sau cả hai vẫn là động từ nguyên (V-bare).
grammarNotes.bodyEn: 'Could' and 'should' are modal verbs. 'Yusuf could send them back' = he is able to do it (possibility). 'He should write no longer at this address' = he is expected to (rule). Both are followed by the bare verb (no -s, no 'to').

translationVi:
Yusuf là một người đưa thư ở Cairo. Anh đưa thư cùng một khu phố mỗi ngày. Anh làm việc này hai mươi hai năm rồi.

Trên phố của anh có một tòa nhà tên Số 14. Tòa nhà đã đóng. Chủ nhà đi Canada hai năm trước. Bây giờ không ai sống ở đó.

Nhưng thư vẫn đến. Sáu hay bảy lá một tháng. Hóa đơn. Thiệp từ bạn cũ. Một tờ tạp chí. Đôi khi là một thiệp cưới.

Yusuf có thể gửi trả. Bưu điện có quy định cho việc đó. Anh nên viết 'không còn ở địa chỉ này' và gửi trả lại.

Anh không làm vậy.

Anh bỏ vào một cái hộp nhỏ phía sau quầy ở bưu điện. Anh có một cái hộp cho Số 14. Anh cũng có một hộp cho Số 27, một ông cụ đã chuyển đến Alexandria. Và một hộp cho người phụ nữ ở Số 9, người đã mất mùa đông năm ngoái.

Một hôm, một cô gái trẻ đến bưu điện. Cô là con gái của người phụ nữ ở Số 9. Cô đến từ Đức.

Yusuf đưa cô cái hộp.

Cô ngồi trên bậc thềm bên ngoài. Cô đọc từng lá thư. Có thư làm cô khóc. Có thư làm cô cười. Sau hai giờ, cô quay lại và hỏi: 'Sao anh biết tôi sẽ đến?'

Yusuf đáp: 'Tôi không biết. Nhưng có ai đó có thể đến. Và nếu không ai đến, thư vẫn ở nơi chúng vốn ở. Đó là cách trung thực hơn so với thùng rác.'

questions.prompt: How long has Yusuf been a postman?
questions.explanation: 'He has done this for twenty-two years.'

options: The owner went to Canada two years ago
questions.explanation: 'The owner went to Canada two years ago.'

questions.prompt: What kinds of letters still come to Number 14?
options: Bills, cards from old friends, a magazine, sometimes a wedding invitation

questions.prompt: According to the post office rule, what should Yusuf do?
options: Write 'no longer at this address' and return the letters
questions.explanation: 'He should write no longer at this address and return them.'

questions.prompt: What does Yusuf actually do with the letters?
options: Puts them in a small box behind his counter
Returns them all to the sender
Burns them at the end of the year
questions.explanation: 'He puts them in a small box behind his counter.'
questions.hint: After the post office rule is described.

questions.prompt: How many boxes does the passage mention by name?
questions.explanation: He keeps a box for each absent person — three are named.

questions.prompt: Who came to pick up a box one day?
options: The daughter of the woman from Number 9, from Germany
questions.explanation: 'She was the daughter of the woman from Number 9. She had come from Germany.'

questions.prompt: What did the young woman do with the letters?
options: She sat on the steps outside and read each one — some made her cry, some made her smile
She took them home without opening them
questions.hint: Right after she received the box.

questions.prompt: Why does Yusuf keep the letters?
options: Somebody might come — keeping them is more honest than the bin
He hopes to read them himself
He is paid extra for it
questions.explanation: 'And if nobody comes, the letters are still where they were. That is more honest than the bin.'

options: A worker's quiet decision to bend a rule can preserve human connections the system would erase
Cairo has too many empty buildings
questions.explanation: Yusuf's whole behaviour points to this.
questions.hint: Think about the rule vs his choice.

Yusuf is a postman in {{b1}}. He delivers letters in the same neighbourhood every day. He has done this for twenty-two years.

On his street there is a building called Number 14. The building is {{b2}}. The owner went to Canada two years ago. No one lives there now.

But the letters still come. Six or seven a {{b3}}. Bills. Cards from old friends. A magazine. Sometimes a wedding invitation.

Yusuf could send them back. The post office has a {{b4}} for that. He should write 'no longer at this address' and return them.

He does not.

He puts them in a small box behind his {{b5}} at the post office. He has a box for Number 14. He also has a box for Number 27, an old man who moved to Alexandria. And a box for the {{b6}} from Number 9, who died last winter.

One day, a young woman came to the post office. She was the daughter of the woman from Number 9. She had come from {{b7}}.

Yusuf gave her the box.

She sat on the steps outside. She read each letter. Some made her {{b8}}. Some made her smile. After two hours, she came back and said, 'How did you know I would come?'

Yusuf said, 'I did not know. But somebody {{b9}}. And if nobody comes, the letters are still where they were. That is more honest than the {{b10}}.'

criticalThinkingQuestion: Yusuf calls keeping the letters 'more honest than the bin.' Think of a small rule at your work or school that, if you followed it exactly, would erase something quietly meaningful. What would 'more honest' look like in that situation?
